html:
[1262]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 鬼呀然一声去 - Vivid, onomatopoeic expression meaning 'the ghost suddenly made a sound and disappeared.'
  • 白昼与客坐 - Literally 'to sit with guests during the day,' but can be understood as 'to host guests during the day.'
  • 揣君耆德硕学 - Polite way of saying 'considering your advanced age and great learning.'
  • 不虞其仍现形也 - 'Did not expect it to still appear in its true form,' indicating the owner's surprise.
  • 多以月下行院中 - 'Often walks around the courtyard at night,' describing the ghost's nocturnal activity.
  • 掩面急走 - Vivid description of people covering their faces and rushing away upon seeing the ghost.
  • 面上仍墨污狼藉 - 'Still had ink stains all over its face,' describing the ghost's appearance.
  • 有形无质 - 'Having form but no substance,' referring to the ghost's ethereal nature.
  • 久成精魅 - 'Long ago became a spirit or demon,' indicating that the ghost was once a human who transformed into a supernatural being.
  • 借婢幻形 - 'Took the form of a deceased servant,' explaining how the ghost appeared.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鬼 - Refers to a supernatural entity, commonly found in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 婢 - Refers to a female servant, often associated with domestic work and lower social status.
  • 白昼 - Refers to daytime, contrasting with the ghost's nocturnal activity.
  • 酉阳杂俎 - A famous collection of strange stories and anecdotes from the Tang Dynasty, often cited in classical Chinese literature.
  • 郭元振 - A historical figure from the Tang Dynasty, known for his military and political achievements.
  • 山居 - Refers to living in the mountains, often associated with seclusion and tranquility.
  • 染翰 - A literary term meaning 'to write with a brush,' often used in classical Chinese literature to describe the act of writing.
  • 久戍人偏老 - Phrase from the Tang Dynasty poem, meaning 'those who have been stationed for a long time grow old,' used to describe the ghost's aged appearance.
  • 长征马不肥 - Phrase from the same poem, meaning 'horses that have traveled long distances do not become fat,' used to describe the ghost's emaciated state.

Proper Nouns

  • 鬼 - The supernatural entity.
  • 婢 - The deceased servant.
  • 酉阳杂俎 - The collection of strange stories.
  • 郭元振 - The historical figure from the Tang Dynasty.
  • 久戍人偏老，长征马不肥 - Phrases from the Tang Dynasty poem.

[1263]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 就水灌田 - Literal phrase meaning 'irrigate fields with water,' describing a common agricultural practice.
  • 就田起屋 - Means 'build houses near the fields,' indicating the close relationship between farming and habitation.
  • 不能比闾而居 - Literally 'cannot live side by side in rows,' suggesting a dispersed settlement pattern.
  • 自筑数椽 - Means 'build a few rooms themselves,' describing the construction of small, simple dwellings.
  • 四无邻舍 - Literally 'no neighbors around,' emphasizing isolation.
  • 如杜工部诗所谓一家村者 - A reference to a poem by Du Fu (杜甫), a famous Tang dynasty poet, where he describes a village with a single family.
  • 人无徭役 - Means 'no corvée labor,' indicating a lack of compulsory labor obligations.
  • 地无丈量 - Means 'no land measurement,' suggesting that the land is not officially surveyed or taxed.
  • 纳三十亩之税，即可坐耕数百亩之产 - Literal phrase meaning 'paying tax on thirty acres allows one to farm hundreds of acres,' highlighting the economic arrangement.
  • 深岩穷谷 - Literally 'deep rocks and remote valleys,' describing remote and rugged areas.
  • 入山行猎 - Means 'go into the mountains to hunt,' describing the activity of hunting.
  • 门户坚闭 - Means 'doors and gates tightly closed,' indicating that the house is securely shut.
  • 院中似有十余马 - Literally 'in the courtyard there seem to be over ten horses,' describing the presence of many horses.
  • 鞍辔悉具 - Means 'saddles and bridles are all prepared,' indicating that the horses are ready for use.
  • 度必玛哈沁所据 - Literally 'it is likely that the Makhans have taken over,' suggesting a suspicion of Makhans (a term for certain nomadic groups).
  • 噪而围之 - Means 'shouting and surrounding them,' describing the action of the soldiers.
  • 玛哈沁见势众，弃锅帐突围去 - Literally 'the Makhans saw the large number and abandoned their pots and tents to break out,' describing their reaction to the attack.
  • 众惮其死斗，亦遂不追 - Literally 'the soldiers feared their fierce fighting and did not pursue,' indicating the soldiers' reluctance to continue the chase.
  • 骸骨狼籍 - Means 'bones scattered,' describing the scene of disarray.
  • 寂无一人 - Means 'silent and no one there,' indicating the absence of people.
  • 隐隐有泣声 - Literally 'a faint sound of weeping,' describing a subtle indication of distress.
  • 寻视见幼童约十三四 - Literally 'searching and seeing a child about thirteen or fourteen years old,' describing the discovery of a young boy.
  • 裸体悬窗棂上 - Means 'naked hanging from the window frame,' describing the boy's condition.
  • 解缚问之 - Means 'untie and ask him,' describing the act of freeing and questioning the boy.
  • 玛哈沁四日前来 - Literally 'the Makhans came four days ago,' indicating the timing of their arrival.
  • 父兄与斗不胜 - Means 'the father and brothers fought but were defeated,' describing the outcome of the initial conflict.
  • 即一家并被缚 - Literally 'the whole family was bound,' describing the capture of the family.
  • 率一日牵二人至山溪洗濯曳归 - Literally 'daily taking two people to the mountain stream to wash and drag back,' describing the systematic abuse.
  • 共脔割炙食 - Means 'cut into pieces and roasted to eat,' describing the brutal treatment.
  • 男妇七八人并尽矣 - Literally 'seven or eight men and women were all killed,' indicating the number of victims.
  • 今日临行 - Literally 'today when they were about to leave,' indicating the timing of the last act.
  • 洗濯我毕 - Literally 'washed me and finished,' describing the boy's ordeal.
  • 将就食 - Means 'were about to eat,' indicating the next step in their plan.
  • 中一人摇手止之 - Literally 'one person shook his hand to stop them,' describing an intervention.
  • 虽不解额鲁特语 - Means 'although I do not understand the Oirat language,' indicating the translator's lack of knowledge of the language.
  • 观其指画 - Means 'observing their gestures,' describing the method of communication.
  • 似欲支解为数段 - Literally 'seemed to want to cut into several pieces,' describing the intended action.
  • 各携于马上为粮 - Means 'each to carry on their horses as food,' describing the intended use of the boy.
  • 幸兵至弃去 - Literally 'fortunately the soldiers arrived and they abandoned him,' indicating the boy's rescue.
  • 今得更生 - Means 'now I have been saved,' describing the boy's current state.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乌鲁木齐 - Refers to Urumqi, the capital of Xinjiang, China.
  • 杜工部 - Refers to Du Fu, a renowned Tang dynasty poet known for his realistic and socially conscious poetry.
  • 玛哈沁 - Refers to Makhans, a term often used to describe certain nomadic groups, possibly referring to Kazakhs or other Turkic peoples.
  • 额鲁特 - Refers to the Oirats, a group of Mongolic peoples, including the Kalmyks and the Dzungars.
  • 吉木萨 - Refers to Yili (伊犁), a region in Xinjiang known for its historical significance and military presence.

Proper Nouns

  • 乌鲁木齐 - Urumqi, the capital of Xinjiang.
  • 杜工部 - Du Fu, a famous Tang dynasty poet.
  • 玛哈沁 - Makhans, a term for certain nomadic groups.
  • 额鲁特 - Oirats, a group of Mongolic peoples.
  • 吉木萨 - Yili, a region in Xinjiang.

[1264]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 泣絮絮不止 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'weeping incessantly,' emphasizing the continuous nature of the weeping.
  • 引归营中姑使执杂役 - Elliptical phrase meaning 'led back to the camp to temporarily assign them to menial tasks.'
  • 埋窖中 - Means 'buried in a pit,' referring to the hiding place of the treasure.
  • 皔见一二车孤行 - Means 'seeing one or two carts traveling alone,' describing the scenario where the bandits attacked.
  • 突起杀其人 - Means 'suddenly rise and kill the people,' indicating the sudden and violent nature of the attack.
  • 即以车载尸入深山 - Means 'immediately load the bodies onto the cart and take them into the deep mountains,' describing the method of disposal.
  • 合手以巨斧碎之 - Means 'gather and smash with a large axe,' indicating the violent destruction of the bodies.
  • 与尸及幞被并投于绝涧 - Means 'along with the bodies and wrapped bedding, throw them into the sheer ravine,' describing the final disposal method.
  • 惟以马驮货去 - Means 'only use horses to carry the goods away,' indicating the method of transporting the loot.
  • 再至马不能通 - Means 'when the horses can no longer pass,' describing the point at which the horses could not continue.
  • 投羁绁于绝涧 - Means 'throw the reins and bridles into the sheer ravine,' indicating the disposal of the horses' equipment.
  • 纵马任其所往 - Means 'let the horses go wherever they will,' indicating the abandonment of the horses.
  • 共负之由鸟道归 - Means 'carry it together along the bird's path back,' describing the difficult journey back.
  • 计去行劫处数百里矣 - Means 'the distance from the place of the robbery is hundreds of miles,' indicating the great distance covered.
  • 归而窖藏一两年 - Means 'return and hide it in a pit for one or two years,' describing the hiding of the treasure.
  • 使人伪为商贩 - Means 'send someone to pretend to be a merchant,' indicating the method of selling the goods.
  • 绕道至辟展诸处卖于市 - Means 'take a detour to various places like Biezhuan and sell in the market,' describing the method of selling the goods.
  • 不虞玛哈沁之灭其门也 - Means 'without suspecting that the Mahaqin would destroy their family,' indicating the unexpected outcome.
  • 童子以幼免连坐 - Means 'the child was young and thus exempt from collective punishment,' indicating the child's exemption.
  • 后亦牧马坠崖死 - Means 'later, while herding horses, he fell off a cliff and died,' describing the child's tragic fate.
  • 遂无遗种 - Means 'and thus there were no surviving descendants,' indicating the end of the family line.
  • 此事余在军幕所经理 - Means 'this matter was managed by me while I was in the military camp,' indicating the narrator's role.
  • 以盗已死遂置无论 - Means 'because the thief was already dead, it was put aside and not pursued,' indicating the reason for not pursuing the matter.
  • 此盗踪迹诡秘 - Means 'this thief's tracks were mysterious,' indicating the difficulty in tracking the thief.
  • 猝不易缉 - Means 'it was not easy to catch suddenly,' indicating the challenge in apprehending the thief.
  • 乃有玛哈沁来 - Means 'then the Mahaqin came,' indicating the unexpected appearance of the Mahaqin.
  • 以报其惨杀之罪 - Means 'to avenge the cruel murder,' indicating the purpose of the Mahaqin's actions.
  • 玛哈沁食人无餍 - Means 'the Mahaqin have no limit to their appetite for human flesh,' indicating the Mahaqin's nature.
  • 乃留一童子 - Means 'they left one child,' indicating the sparing of the child.
  • 以明其召祸之由 - Means 'to show the cause of their calamity,' indicating the reason for sparing the child.
  • 此中似有神理 - Means 'there seems to be a divine principle at work here,' indicating the narrator's belief in a higher purpose.
  • 非偶然也 - Means 'it is not accidental,' reinforcing the idea of a higher purpose.
  • 盗姓名久忘 - Means 'the thief's name has long been forgotten,' indicating the loss of the thief's identity.
  • 惟童子坠崖时 - Means 'only when the child fell off the cliff,' indicating the specific moment.
  • 所司牒报记名秋儿云 - Means 'the official report recorded the name as Qiu'er,' indicating the child's name in the official records.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 玛哈沁 - Refers to a group of nomadic people known for their ferocity and cannibalism, often mentioned in historical accounts of the region.
  • 辟展 - Refers to a place name, likely a market town or trading post in the region.
  • 连坐 - A form of collective punishment where the family or associates of a criminal are punished along with the criminal.
  • 鸟道 - A term used to describe a difficult or dangerous path, often associated with mountainous or rugged terrain.

Proper Nouns

  • 玛哈沁 - The group of nomadic people.
  • 辟展 - The place name.
  • 秋儿 - The child's name in the official records.

[1266]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 因忆 - Means 'upon recalling' or 'as I remember,' used to introduce a recollection.
  • 俗谓之 - Means 'commonly called' or 'referred to as,' indicating a popular name or term.
  • 督闽浙 - Means 'overseeing Fujian and Zhejiang,' referring to the administrative role of the official.
  • 慎勿怖 - Means 'do not be afraid,' a direct warning to avoid fear.
  • 不为害也 - Means 'it will not cause harm,' assuring the listener that the sight is harmless.
  • 已下键睡 - Means 'had already locked the door and gone to sleep,' describing the action of securing the room and retiring.
  • 因天暑 - Means 'because of the heat,' providing a reason for the action.
  • 移床近窗 - Means 'moved the bed closer to the window,' describing the action taken due to the heat.
  • 隔纱幌视天晴阴 - Means 'looked through the gauze curtain at the sky's brightness or cloudiness,' describing the action of observing the weather.
  • 时虽月黑 - Means 'even though it was dark at night,' indicating the time of day.
  • 檐挂六灯尚未烬 - Means 'the six lamps hanging from the eaves had not yet burned out,' describing the lighting conditions.
  • 见院中黑影 - Means 'saw a black shadow in the courtyard,' describing what was observed.
  • 略似人形 - Means 'slightly resembling a human form,' describing the appearance of the shadow.
  • 在阶前或坐或卧或行或立 - Means 'in front of the steps, either sitting, lying down, walking, or standing,' describing the movements of the shadow.
  • 寂然无一声 - Means 'silent without a sound,' emphasizing the eerie quietness.
  • 夜半再视之 - Means 'looked again at midnight,' describing the repeated observation.
  • 仍在 - Means 'it was still there,' confirming the persistence of the shadow.
  • 至鸡鸣乃渐渐缩入地 - Means 'until the rooster crowed, it gradually receded into the ground,' describing the final disappearance of the shadow.
  • 试问驿吏 - Means 'tried to ask the post official,' indicating an attempt to seek information.
  • 均不知也 - Means 'none knew about it,' indicating the lack of knowledge among the officials.
  • 公为使相 - Means 'you are an envoy and chancellor,' addressing the official's high rank.
  • 当有鬼神为阴从 - Means 'you should have ghosts and spirits as your unseen attendants,' suggesting the official's elevated status.
  • 余焉有是 - Means 'how could I have that,' expressing disbelief.
  • 不然 - Means 'not so,' a negation of the previous statement.
  • 仙霞关内 - Refers to the Xianxia Pass, a strategic location.
  • 水陆要冲 - Means 'a crucial point of land and water transport,' indicating its importance.
  • 用兵者所必争 - Means 'a place that must be contested by those who use military force,' emphasizing its strategic value.
  • 明季唐王 - Refers to the late Ming dynasty and the Prince of Tang.
  • 国初郑氏耿氏 - Refers to the early Qing dynasty and the Zheng and Geng families.
  • 战斗杀伤 - Means 'battles and casualties,' describing the violence.
  • 不知其几 - Means 'cannot be counted,' indicating the large number of casualties.
  • 乘室宇空虚而窃据 - Means 'taking advantage of the emptiness of the buildings to occupy them,' describing the spirits' behavior.
  • 有大官来则避而出耳 - Means 'when a high-ranking official arrives, they hide and leave,' explaining the spirits' reaction.
  • 无处无鬼之说 - Means 'the idea that there are ghosts everywhere,' summarizing the belief.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 福建 - Refers to the province of Fujian in China.
  • 盩关 - Refers to the Zouguan Pass, a specific location within Fujian.
  • 水口 - Refers to a common name for the Zouguan Pass.
  • 大学士 - Refers to a high-ranking scholar-official in the imperial court.
  • 杨公 - Refers to a specific official named Yang.
  • 督闽浙 - Indicates the official's role as the overseer of Fujian and Zhejiang provinces.
  • 明季 - Refers to the late Ming dynasty.
  • 唐王 - Refers to the Prince of Tang, a title given to certain members of the royal family during the Ming dynasty.
  • 国初 - Refers to the early Qing dynasty.
  • 郑氏 - Refers to the Zheng family, a prominent family during the early Qing dynasty.
  • 耿氏 - Refers to the Geng family, another prominent family during the early Qing dynasty.
  • 仙霞关 - Refers to the Xianxia Pass, a strategic location in Fujian.
  • 水陆要冲 - Indicates the importance of the location as a key transportation hub.
  • 用兵者所必争 - Highlights the strategic value of the location in military conflicts.
  • 战斗杀伤 - Describes the violence and casualties that occurred in the region.
  • 乘室宇空虚而窃据 - Describes the behavior of spirits taking advantage of abandoned buildings.
  • 有大官来则避而出耳 - Explains the spirits' reaction to the presence of high-ranking officials.

Proper Nouns

  • 福建 - Province of Fujian.
  • 盩关 - Zouguan Pass.
  • 水口 - Common name for the Zouguan Pass.
  • 大学士 - High-ranking scholar-official.
  • 杨公 - Official named Yang.
  • 督闽浙 - Role of overseeing Fujian and Zhejiang.
  • 明季 - Late Ming dynasty.
  • 唐王 - Prince of Tang.
  • 国初 - Early Qing dynasty.
  • 郑氏 - Zheng family.
  • 耿氏 - Geng family.
  • 仙霞关 - Xianxia Pass.
  • 水陆要冲 - Crucial point of land and water transport.
  • 用兵者所必争 - Place that must be contested by military forces.
  • 战斗杀伤 - Battles and casualties.
  • 乘室宇空虚而窃据 - Taking advantage of abandoned buildings.
  • 有大官来则避而出耳 - Hiding and leaving when a high-ranking official arrives.
  • 无处无鬼之说 - Idea that there are ghosts everywhere.

[1267]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 老仆 - Refers to an elderly servant, a common term in classical Chinese literature.
  • 据之室 - Literally 'the room they occupy,' but contextually it refers to a house or dwelling.
  • 暂让之何害 - Means 'what harm is there in temporarily letting them stay?'
  • 必出扰之 - Means 'why must they necessarily disturb them?'
  • 遇禄命重、血气刚者 - Refers to people with strong fortunes or robust personalities.
  • 自败 - Means 'self-destruct' or 'bring about their own downfall.'
  • 符录劾治 - Refers to the use of talismans and exorcism rituals, often associated with Daoist practices.
  • 更蹈不测 - Means 'even face unforeseen dangers.'
  • 人既不居，屋必不葺 - Means 'since no one lives there, the house will not be maintained.'
  • 久而自圯 - Means 'over time, it will collapse on its own.'
  • 汝又何归耶 - Means 'where will you go then?'
  • 内倚廖媪 - Refers to relying on an elderly woman named Liao, likely a trusted servant.
  • 井井 - Means 'neatly organized' or 'well-managed.'
  • 元夜 - Refers to the first night of the first lunar month, commonly known as the Lantern Festival.
  • 游灯市 - Means 'to visit the lantern market.'
  • 键诸箧 - Means 'to lock them in a chest.'
  • 细事 - Means 'trivial matters.'
  • 言人所难言也 - Means 'to speak what others find difficult to say.'
  • 徘徊四顾 - Means 'to wander around and look around.'
  • 远想慨然 - Means 'to feel a sense of loss or nostalgia.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鬼 - Refers to spirits or ghosts, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 符录劾治 - Refers to Daoist exorcism practices, which were widely believed in ancient China.
  • 太夫人 - Refers to a respected lady, often a mother or grandmother.
  • 四官 - Likely a nickname or title for the author or a family member.
  • 元夜 - The Lantern Festival, a significant cultural event in Chinese tradition.
  • 千字文 - A classic educational text used for teaching literacy in ancient China.
  • 姚安公 - Likely a title or nickname for a respected elder in the family.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This phrase is elliptical but means 'spirits occupy the house, and people rarely go there.'
  • This phrase is also elliptical but means 'occasionally a guest might stay, but only temporarily.'
  • This long sentence can be broken down into a series of logical steps: 'If not, since no one lives there, the house will not be maintained, and over time it will collapse on its own, where will you go then?'
  • This long sentence describes the background of the servant Xi, emphasizing his slow progress in learning.
  • This phrase is elliptical but means 'however, he is naturally loyal and straightforward, treating the master's affairs as his own, even if they are disliked or resented.'

Proper Nouns

  • 施祥 - The name of the servant who speaks about ghosts.
  • 刘文斗 - The name of another servant who comments on the first servant's statement.
  • 姚安公 - A respected elder in the family.
  • 太夫人 - A respected lady, likely the mother or grandmother of the author.
  • 四官 - A nickname or title for the author or a family member.
  • 廖媪 - The name of an elderly woman who is trusted and relied upon in the household.

[1269]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 姚安公言 - Phrase indicating that the following story is told by 'Yao'an Gong', likely a historical figure or a respected elder.
  • 谒选时 - Refers to the period when a scholar-official candidate is waiting to be selected for an official position.
  • 短盘 - Term used to describe the practice of traveling by hiring a donkey for short distances, often used by poor scholars.
  • 题缺 - Refers to the position or vacancy in an official post.
  • 忠厚养福 - Phrase meaning 'to cultivate blessings through kindness and integrity'.
  • 无故坐此 - Literally means 'to sit here without reason', but in context, it implies 'to sit here without permission'.
  • 惶愧自悔 - Means 'to feel ashamed and regretful'.
  • 谋卖屋移家 - Means 'to plan to sell the house and move the family'.
  • 遽掷以块 - Means 'to throw a stone hastily'.
  • 夜夜来此 - Means 'to come here every night'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 庐江孙起山先生 - Refers to Mr. Sun Qishan from Lujiang, a historical figure or a respected scholar.
  • 河间 - Refers to Hejian, a place name in modern-day Hebei Province.
  • 起山入都 - Means 'Qishan entered the capital', indicating that he moved to the capital city to seek an official position.
  • 河间犹未改题缺 - Means 'the position in Hejian was still vacant', indicating that the vacancy had not been filled yet.
  • 狐魅 - Refers to fox spirits, a common motif in Chinese folklore, often associated with supernatural events.

Proper Nouns

  • 姚安公 - A historical figure or a respected elder.
  • 庐江孙起山先生 - Mr. Sun Qishan from Lujiang.
  • 河间 - Hejian, a place name in modern-day Hebei Province.
  • 起山 - Sun Qishan.
  • 狐魅 - Fox spirits.

[1271]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夫词人引用，渔猎百家 - Common phrase meaning 'when scholars cite, they gather from a hundred schools,' indicating the practice of drawing from diverse sources.
  • 原不能一一核实 - Means 'originally cannot be verified one by one,' acknowledging the difficulty of verifying all citations.
  • 过于诬罔 - Means 'exceedingly false and misleading,' criticizing the distortion of facts.
  • 庄列寓言 - Refers to the allegorical stories in the works of Zhuangzi and Liezi, which often use fiction to convey philosophical ideas.
  • 战国诸子 - Refers to the philosophers of the Warring States period, known for their diverse and sometimes contradictory views.
  • 谶纬稗官 - Refers to apocryphal texts and minor officials' records, often containing superstitions and unverified claims.
  • 递相祖述 - Means 'passed down and followed each other,' indicating a chain of transmission and imitation.
  • 肆无忌惮 - Means 'reckless and without restraint,' describing a lack of moral or ethical boundaries.
  • 李冗独异志 - Refers to a work by Li Rong, which contains unusual and controversial claims.
  • 伏羲兄妹为夫妇 - Refers to the mythological story of Fuxi and his sister Nüwa being married.
  • 张华博物志 - Refers to a work by Zhang Hua, which includes various natural and cultural observations.
  • 尼山 - Refers to Mount Ni, where Confucius is said to have been born.
  • 大儒为龙阳 - A derogatory term implying that a great scholar is a homosexual.
  • 汉书贾谊传 - Refers to the biography of Jia Yi in the *Book of Han*.
  • 太守吴公爱幸之 - Means 'Governor Wu Gong loved and favored him,' referring to Jia Yi's relationship with Governor Wu.
  • 骈语雕龙 - Refers to a book on rhetoric and literary composition.
  • 晁以道诗 - Refers to a poem by Chao Yidao.
  • 杀翁分我一杯羹 - A phrase from Chao Yidao's poem, suggesting that Liu Bang (Emperor Gaozu) was born from a dragon and not his father.
  • 龙种由来事杳冥 - Means 'the dragon offspring has always been a mysterious matter,' reinforcing the idea of Liu Bang's divine origin.
  • 成风私事季友 - Refers to a historical event where Chengfeng had a private relationship with Ji You.
  • 敬嬴私事襄仲 - Refers to a similar event involving Jing Ying and Xiang Zhong.
  • 私字 - Refers to the character '私,' which means 'private' or 'secret.'

[1272]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 從叔梅庵公 - Refers to the author's uncle, who is the narrator of the story.
  • 聞某墓中有狐迹 - Colloquial way of saying that there were signs of fox activity in a certain tomb.
  • 夜携铳往 - Direct and literal translation, meaning 'at night, they took a gun and went'.
  • 共伏草中伺之 - Describes their actions in a straightforward manner, meaning 'they both hid in the grass and waited for it'.
  • 以背相倚而睡 - Vivid description of their position, meaning 'they slept with their backs leaning against each other'.
  • 醒则两人之发交结为一，贯穿缭绕 - Detailed and somewhat poetic description of their entanglement, meaning 'when they woke up, their hair was intertwined into one, passing through and winding around each other'.
  • 猝不可解 - Indicates the suddenness and difficulty of the situation, meaning 'suddenly, they could not be untangled'.
  • 互相牵掣不能行，亦不能立 - Describes the physical struggle, meaning 'they were pulling each other and could neither move nor stand'.
  • 稍稍转动，即彼此呼痛 - Vivid depiction of their discomfort, meaning 'slightly moving, they both cried out in pain'.
  • 胶扰彻晓 - Emphasizes the duration of their entanglement, meaning 'they were stuck together all night'.
  • 望见行路者，始呼至，断以佩刀 - Clear sequence of events, meaning 'seeing a passerby, they called for help, and the passerby cut them apart with a dagger'.
  • 愤欲往报 - Expresses their initial reaction, meaning 'they were angry and wanted to go back to report'.
  • 父老曰 - Introduces the advice given by the older generation, meaning 'the elders said'.
  • 彼无形声，非力所胜 - Metaphorical description of the foxes' nature, meaning 'they have no form or sound, and cannot be overcome by force'.
  • 且无故而侵彼，理亦不直 - Moral argument, meaning 'moreover, without reason to invade them, the logic is not right'.
  • 侮实自召，又何仇焉 - Rhetorical question emphasizing the folly of retaliation, meaning 'the insult was self-invited, so why harbor resentment?'.
  • 仇必败滋甚 - Warning about the consequences of revenge, meaning 'retaliation will only lead to greater failure'.
  • 二人乃止 - Indicates the resolution of the conflict, meaning 'the two then stopped'.
  • 此狐小虐之使警 - Explains the purpose of the foxes' actions, meaning 'this was a small torment to make them cautious'.
  • 不深创之，以激其必报 - Strategic consideration, meaning 'without deeply harming them, to provoke a certain revenge'.
  • 亦可谓善自全矣 - Final evaluation, meaning 'it can also be said that they were good at preserving themselves'.
  • 然小虐亦足以激怒 - Counterpoint to the previous evaluation, meaning 'however, even a small torment can provoke anger'.
  • 不如敛戢勿动，使伺之无迹弥善也 - Concluding piece of advice, meaning 'it is better to restrain oneself and not act, making sure there are no traces to be sought even better'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 从叔梅庵公 - The author's uncle, who is the narrator of the story.
  • 狐迹 - Fox tracks, which in Chinese folklore often symbolize supernatural or magical elements.
  • 铳 - A firearm, indicating a time period when firearms were in use.
  • 佩刀 - A dagger, a common weapon in historical Chinese narratives.
  • 父老 - Elders or wise old men, often respected figures in traditional Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

[1276]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 华州试月中桂诗举许棠为第一人 - The poetry contest in Huazhou, where Xu Tang was selected as the first-place winner for his poem about the moonlit osmanthus.
  • 棠诗今不传 - Xu Tang's poem is no longer transmitted, meaning it is lost or not widely known.
  • 非曾见王定保摭言、计敏夫唐诗纪事者不知也 - Those who have not seen Wang Dingbao's *Zhiyan* or Ji Minfu's *Tangshi Jishi* would not know, indicating that these works are the primary sources for the information.
  • 中彼卷之开花临上界，持斧有仙郎，何如中此诗乎 - How does the poem in the other scroll, which speaks of flowers blooming in the upper realm and a celestial youth holding an axe, compare to this poem?
  • 微公拨入，亦自愿易之 - If it were not for your intervention, I would have willingly changed it, indicating a voluntary change due to external influence.
  • 余丙子扈从古北口时，车马拥塞，就旅舍小憩，见壁上一诗，剥残过半，惟三四句可辨，最爱其一水涨喧人语外，万山青到马蹄前二语 - During the year of Bingzi, when I was accompanying the emperor to the north of the Great Wall, the carriages and horses were congested. I took a rest in a hostel and saw a poem on the wall, half of which was worn away, but only a few lines were still legible. I loved the lines 'The water rises, and the human voices grow distant; the green mountains stretch to the hooves of the horses.'
  • 以为云中路绕巴山色，树里河流汉水声，不是过也 - I thought that 'The road in the clouds winds around the color of Bashan, and the trees hide the sound of the Han River flowing,' was not an exaggeration.
  • 惜不得姓名，及展其卷，此诗在焉，乃知芥契合，已在六七年前，相与叹息者久之 - I regretted not knowing the author's name, but when I opened his scroll, this poem was there. Thus, I realized that our affinity had been present for six or seven years, and we sighed together for a long time.
  • 子颖待余最尽礼，殁后，其二子承父之志，见余尚依依有情 - Zhu Ziyi treated me with the utmost courtesy, and after his death, his two sons continued his legacy, and I still felt a deep affection for them.
  • 翰墨因缘，良非偶尔 - The connection through writing and calligraphy was indeed not accidental.
  • 何尝以拨房为亲疏哉 - How could I ever consider the selection of rooms as a measure of closeness or distance? This is a rhetorical question emphasizing the importance of content over form.
  • 余严江舟中诗曰：山色空濛淡似烟，参差绿到大江边，斜阳流水推篷坐，处处随人欲上船 - My poem on the boat in the Yangtze River says: 'The mountain colors are hazy and light as smoke, the uneven green reaches the edge of the great river, the setting sun and flowing water urge me to sit under the awning, and everywhere I go, I want to board a boat.'
  • 实从万山句夺胎 - This is indeed derived from the line 'The green mountains stretch to the hooves of the horses.'
  • 尝以语子颖曰：人言青出于蓝，今日乃蓝出于青 - I once told Zhu Ziyi: 'People say that blue comes from indigo, but today it is the indigo that comes from the blue.'
  • 子颖虽逊谢，意似默可 - Zhu Ziyi humbly declined, but his expression seemed to silently agree.
  • 此亦诗坛之佳话，并附录于此 - This is also a fine story in the poetry world, and I will record it here.

  • 忽楼窗六扇，无风自开，俄又自阖 - A vivid description of windows opening and closing without wind, likely a supernatural event.
  • 爱公视之，曰 - A formal introduction of the narrator's observation and statement.
  • 有一僧坐北牖上 - A specific description of a monk sitting on the north window.
  • 其面横阔，须癕癕如久未剃 - A detailed physical description of the monk's face and beard.
  • 目瞪视而项微偻 - A description of the monk's eyes and posture, suggesting a ghostly presence.
  • 盖缢鬼也 - A conclusion that the monk is a hanged ghost.
  • 以问寺僧，僧不能讳 - A narrative of questioning the temple monks, who cannot deny the truth.
  • 惟怪何以识其貌，疑有人泄之 - The monks' reaction, questioning how the narrator recognized the ghost's appearance and suspecting someone leaked the information.
  • 不知爱公之自能视也 - A revelation that the narrator has the ability to see ghosts.
  • 又偶在船头，戏拈篙刺水 - A description of the narrator playing with a pole in the boat.
  • 忽掷篙却避，面有惊色 - A sudden reaction of fear, suggesting a ghostly encounter.
  • 怪诘其故，曰 - The narrator's questioning and the response.
  • 有溺鬼缘篙欲上也 - The explanation that a drowning ghost was climbing the pole.
  • 戊午八月，宴蒙古外藩于清音阁 - A specific date and event, a banquet for Mongol envoys at Qingyin Pavilion.
  • 爱公与余连席，余以松岩所语叩之 - The narrator's interaction with the ghost-seer, questioning him about previous encounters.
  • 云皆不妄，然则随处有鬼，亦复如人 - The ghost-seer's confirmation and the narrator's reflection on the ubiquity of ghosts.
  • 此求归之鬼，有系恋心 - A description of a ghost seeking return, with a sense of attachment.
  • 开窗之鬼，有争据心 - A description of a ghost trying to occupy a space, with a sense of competition.
  • 缘篙之鬼，有竞斗心 - A description of a ghost trying to climb the pole, with a sense of struggle.
  • 其得失胜负，喜怒哀乐，更当一一如人 - A reflection on the emotional states of ghosts, similar to humans.
  • 是胶胶扰扰，地下尚无了期 - A commentary on the chaotic and unresolved nature of the afterlife.
  • 释氏讲忏悔解脱，圣人之法，亦使有所归而不为厉 - A reference to Buddhist teachings and the wisdom of sages, suggesting a way to resolve the chaos.
  • 其深知鬼神之情状矣 - A conclusion that the sages deeply understand the nature of ghosts and spirits.
  • 子贡曰：大哉死乎？君子息焉 - A quotation from Zizigong, emphasizing the significance of death.
  • 庄周曰：嗟来桑扈乎？而已反其真，特就耳目所及言之耳 - A quotation from Zhuangzi, suggesting a return to one's true nature.

  • 莫不掩口 - Literal expression meaning 'no one could help but cover their mouths,' describing the shocked reaction.
  • 士人恚甚，望空指画詈妖狐 - Literal expression meaning 'the scholar was very angry, pointing and cursing the fox in the air,' describing his reaction.
  • 忽檐际大笑曰 - Literal expression meaning 'suddenly, someone laughed loudly from the eaves,' describing the unexpected intervention.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence of clauses describing the speaker's reaction to the addressee's behavior is somewhat elliptical, requiring careful reading to understand the logical flow.
  • The threat and promise in the clause '行且绘此像于君家白板扉，博途人一粲矣' require attention to the implied actions.
  • The sequence describing the scholar preparing for guests, a black object rushing out the door, and the scholar recognizing it as a fox spirit requires attention to the temporal sequence.
  • The sequence describing the crowd's response to the fox spirit, the fox spirit's behavior, and its dietary habits requires attention to the descriptive details.

  • 伏地战栗 - Lying on the ground trembling. Vividly portrays the calf's fear.

  • 畏屠者如初 - Still feared the butcher as before. Shows the calf's persistent fear.
  • 伺屠者侧卧凳上 - Waiting for the butcher to lie down on a bench. Describes the calf's strategic timing.
  • 一触而贯其心 - Stabbed him through the heart. Vividly describes the calf's attack.
  • 逸之竟莫知所往 - Escaped and no one knew where it went. Indicates the calf's disappearance.
  • 遇赦获免 - Freed by amnesty. Refers to the legal process of being released.
  • 同里磑 - Living in the same village. Indicates the familial connection.

[1353]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 山半有飛瀑二丈余 - Describes a waterfall located halfway up the mountain, approximately two zhang (a traditional Chinese unit of length) high.
  • 其泉甚甘 - Indicates that the water from the waterfall is very sweet or pure.
  • 會冬月冰結 - Suggests that during winter, the water freezes into ice.

  • 食之病 - Implies that drinking the river water can cause illness.
  • 仍鑿瀑泉之冰水 - Indicates that people still drill through the ice of the waterfall to obtain water.
  • 竅甫通，即有無數冰丸隨而涌出 - Describes how as soon as a hole is made, countless ice balls emerge.
  • 形皆如橄欖 - Indicates that the shape of the ice balls is like olives.
  • 破之中有白蟲如蠶 - Describes that when broken open, the ice balls contain white worms resembling silkworms.
  • 其口與足則深紅 - Indicates that the mouths and feet of the worms are a deep red color.
  • 殆所謂冰蠶者欤 - Suggests that these might be what are called 'ice worms.'
  • 與鐵中之蟲，煅而不死 - Compares the ice worms to worms found in iron that do not die even when heated.
  • 均可谓异闻矣 - Indicates that both phenomena are considered strange or unusual.
  • 然天地之气，一动一静，互为其根 - Suggests that the movement and stillness of the universe are interdependent and root each other.
  • 极阳之内必伏阴，极阴之内必伏阳 - Indicates that within extreme heat there is latent cold, and within extreme cold there is latent heat.
  • 八卦之对待 - Refers to the eight trigrams in the I Ching (Book of Changes) and their complementary relationships.

[1354]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 坎以二陰包一陽，離以二陽包一陰 - Describes the structure of the Kan (坎) and Li (離) hexagrams in the I Ching (易經). Kan consists of two yin lines enclosing one yang line, while Li consists of two yang lines enclosing one yin line.
  • 陽極於乾，即一陰生 - Refers to the principle of yin and yang transformation, where when yang reaches its extreme (乾, Qian), yin begins to emerge.
  • 陰極於坤，即一陽生 - Similarly, when yin reaches its extreme (坤, Kun), yang begins to emerge.
  • 其靜也伏斯斂，斂斯郁焉 - Describes the process of contraction and stagnation in a state of rest.
  • 其動也郁斯蒸，蒸斯化焉 - Describes the process of expansion and transformation in a state of motion.
  • 至於化則生，生不已矣 - Means that when transformation occurs, life emerges, and this process continues endlessly.
  • 特沖和之氣，其生有常 - Refers to the harmonious and balanced energy (沖和之氣) that produces life in a regular and predictable manner.
  • 偏勝之氣，其生不測 - Refers to the imbalanced energy (偏勝之氣) that produces life in unpredictable ways.
  • 沸鼎炎磓 - Describes a boiling cauldron with intense heat.
  • 寒泉磖結 - Describes a cold spring with frozen water.
  • 其中皆可以生蟲也 - Means that both hot and cold environments can support the growth of insects.
  • 崔豹古今注載火鼠生炎洲火中 - Refers to the Gujin Zhi (古今注) by Cui Bao, which records the existence of fire mice in the fire region (炎洲).
  • 績其毛為布，入火不燃 - Means that the fur of the fire mouse can be spun into cloth that does not burn in fire.
  • 神異經載冰鼠生北海冰中 - Refers to the Shenyi Jing (神異經), which records the existence of ice mice in the ice of the North Sea (北海).
  • 穴冰而居，齧水而食 - Means that the ice mouse lives in ice and feeds on water.
  • 歲久大如象，冰破即死 - Means that over time, the ice mouse grows as large as an elephant and dies when the ice melts.
  • 歐羅巴人曾見之 - Means that Europeans have seen the ice mouse.
  • 謝梅莊前輩戍烏里雅蘇台時，亦曾見之 - Refers to the fact that Xie Meizhuang, a senior official, saw the ice mouse during his time in Uliastai.
  • 其事似異，實則常理也 - Means that although these phenomena seem strange, they are actually consistent with natural principles.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 崔豹 - A scholar who wrote the Gujin Zhi (古今注), a historical and cultural encyclopedia.
  • 神異經 - An ancient Chinese text that records strange and supernatural phenomena.
  • 歐羅巴 - The ancient Chinese term for Europe.
  • 謝梅莊 - A historical figure who served as an official in Uliastai, a region in Inner Mongolia.

Proper Nouns

  • 坎 - The Kan trigram in the I Ching.
  • 離 - The Li trigram in the I Ching.
  • 乾 - The Qian trigram in the I Ching.
  • 坤 - The Kun trigram in the I Ching.
  • 崔豹 - The author of the Gujin Zhi.
  • 神異經 - The title of the ancient text that records strange phenomena.
  • 歐羅巴 - The ancient Chinese term for Europe.

[1356]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 數日後微聞 - A few days later, it was faintly heard; introduces a delayed and vague piece of information.
  • 一日遣二仆 - One day, he sent away two servants; describes a specific action taken by the owner.
  • 罪状不明 - The charges were unclear; indicates that the reasons for the servants' dismissal were not specified.
  • 旋有泄其事者曰 - Soon, someone revealed the matter, saying that...; introduces a subsequent revelation about the incident.
  • 二仆皆谋为司阍 - The two servants both planned to become gatekeepers; describes the servants' intentions.
  • 无如先已有跛者 - But there was already a lame person serving as gatekeeper; explains the obstacle to their plans.
  • 乃各因饰其妇 - So they each tried to beautify their wives; describes the servants' strategy.
  • 俟主人燕息 - Waiting for the master to rest; indicates the timing of their actions.
  • 诱而蛊之 - They lured and bewitched him; describes the servants' method of approach.
  • 至夕，一妇私具饼饵，一妇私煎茶 - By evening, one wife secretly prepared some cakes and pastries, and another wife secretly brewed tea; describes the wives' actions.
  • 皆暗中摸索至书斋廊下 - Both of them groped their way in the dark to the veranda of the study; describes the setting of the encounter.
  • 猝然相触，所赍俱倾 - Suddenly they bumped into each other, and everything they carried fell to the ground; describes the accidental collision.
  • 愧不自容，转怒而相诟 - Embarrassed and unable to bear it, they turned angry and scolded each other; describes the servants' reaction.
  • 主人不欲深究，故善遣去 - The master did not want to investigate deeply, so he kindly dismissed them; explains the master's decision.
  • 此乩可谓灵鬼矣 - This divination can be called a very clever spirit; comments on the accuracy of the divination.
  • 然何以能前知此等事，终无理可推也 - But how could it predict such things in advance? It's ultimately beyond explanation; expresses skepticism about the divination's predictive power.
  • 马夫人雇一针线人，曾在是家 - Madam Ma hired a needlewoman who had previously worked in this household; introduces a new character.
  • 初无自献其妇意 - She had no intention of offering her wife as a gift; clarifies the needlewoman's intentions.
  • 乃私谋于一黠仆 - But she privately plotted with a cunning servant; describes the needlewoman's actions.
  • 黠仆为画此策 - The cunning servant devised this plan; explains the servant's role.
  • 均与约是日有暇，可乘隙以进 - They agreed that on that day they would have free time and could take advantage of the opportunity to approach; describes the plan.
  • 而不使相知 - But they did not let each other know; explains the secrecy of the plan.
  • 故致两败 - Thus, both plans failed; summarizes the outcome.
  • 二仆逐后，黠仆又党附于跛者，邀游妓馆 - After the two servants were dismissed, the cunning servant joined forces with the lame gatekeeper and frequented brothels; describes the servant's subsequent actions.
  • 跛者知其有伏机，阳使先往待，而阴告主人往捕 - The lame gatekeeper knew that the cunning servant had a hidden trap, so he pretended to send him ahead while secretly informing the master to go and catch him; describes the lame gatekeeper's strategy.
  • 故黠仆亦败 - Thus, the cunning servant also failed; summarizes the outcome.
  • 嗟乎！一州县官司阍耳，而此四人者，互相倾轧，至辗转多方而不已 - Alas! A mere gatekeeper for a county official, and yet these four people schemed against each other, going through various twists and turns without end; expresses disappointment in the behavior of the four individuals.
  • 黄雀螳螂之喻，兹其明验矣 - The metaphor of the cicada, the mantis, and the yellow bird is clearly demonstrated here; uses a well-known fable to illustrate the point.
  • 附记之以著世情之险 - I add this note to highlight the dangers of human nature; concludes the passage with a moral lesson.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '今将托钵山东，天下岂有此无良人耶' is a rhetorical question that sets the tone for the narrative, expressing disbelief at the existence of such an unrighteous person.
  • This sequence '方相与太息，忽窗外呜呜有泣声，良久语曰' describes a moment of shared reflection followed by a sudden and emotional interruption.
  • The question '尔尚责人无良耶' challenges the narrator's initial assumption.
  • This long clause '尔家本有妇，见我在门前买花粉，诡言未娶，诳我父母，赘尔于家，尔无良否耶' details a series of actions and accusations, each ending with a rhetorical question.
  • This clause '我父母患疫，先后殁，别无亲属，尔据其宅，收其资，而棺衾祭葬俱草草，与死一奴婢同，尔无良否耶' describes the aftermath of the narrator's parents' death and the narrator's treatment of the deceased, again ending with a rhetorical question.
  • This clause '尔妇附粮艘寻至，入门与尔相诟厉，即欲逐我，既而知原是我家，尔衣食于我，乃暂容留，尔巧说百端，降我为妾，我苟求宁静，忍泪曲从，尔无良否耶' details the narrator's relationship with the narrator's wife and the subsequent reduction to the status of a concubine.
  • This clause '既据我宅，索我供给，又虐使我，呼我小名，动使伏地受杖，尔反代彼揿我项背，按我手足，叱我勿转侧，尔无良否耶' describes the ongoing mistreatment and abuse of the narrator.
